segments.text:
Cyclic Voltammetry
• The electrode potential is ramped linearly to a more positive potential, and then ramped in reverse back to the starting voltage. The forward scan produces a current peak for any analytes that can be oxidized through the range of the potential scan.
Time
V 2
V 1
Voltage

•The current will increase as the potential reaches the oxidation potential of the analyte , but then falls off as the concentration of the analyte is depleted close to the electrode surface .
 Current from an oxidation or reduction is measured as the potential of the working electrode is changed.
• The measured current is limited by analyte diffusion at the electrode surface.
 The measured current is proportional to the analyte concentration in solution.
 The current versus potential graph- voltammogram

A reversible reaction is fast enough to maintain equilibrium concentrations of reactant and product at electrode surface
 Increased separation and broadening of cathodic and anodic peaks occur in irreversible reactions that are not fast enough to maintain equilibrium concentrations at the electrode.

Potentiostat
Instrumentation
•Three electrode cell. Current flows between the working and counter electrodes while the potential of the working electrode is controlled versus the reference electrode.

Working electrode – the one at which the reaction of interest occurs.
Reference electrode – used to measure the potential of the working electrode.
Auxiliary electrode (counter electrode) – current-supporting electrode partner of the working electrode. Current flows between the working and the auxiliary electrodes

Ag/ AgCl electrode: AgCl (s) + e- ⇌ Ag(s) + Cl -
E o = +0.222 V at 25 o C
E (saturated KCl ) = +0.197

Caromel electrode
½ Hg 2 Cl 2 (s) + e - ⇌ Hg(l) + Cl -
E o = +0.268V
E (saturated KCl ) = 0.241 V
